VM to evaluate assertions on these structures [16]. Each
assertion is an invariant that is false only if the application
has been attacked. As an example, an Assurance VM can
compare a hash of the software of an APP-VM against
a value computed ofﬂine to discover anomalous updates.
Other invariants (i) check the integrity of critical kernel data
structures; (ii) assure that no code has been injected into an
application process.
E. Infrastructure Virtual Machines
INF-VMs extend the VMMs to conﬁgure and manage the
overlays. To this purpose, they are interconnected into a dis-
tinct overlay that manages the other overlays and implement
run-time monitoring of the cloud’s resources and update
the mapping of the overlays onto the physical architecture.
The introduction of this overlay minimizes the size of the
VMM by shifting into the VMs of the management overlay
those functions too complex to be developed at the VMM
level. An INF-VM runs a minimal kernel, it does not run
any Internet service and the functionalities it implements
cannot be directly accessed by any user but just by the cloud
administrators.
As shown in Fig. 5, the management overlay does interact
with the other overlays just as side effect of managing the
cloud’s resources. As an example, INF-VMs can:
•create/kill, freeze/resume any VM in their node or
request this operation to another INF-VM;
•conﬁgure a VM according to its template;
Figure 5. Example of Communities and Overlays
•monitor the resource usage resulting from the current
mapping and check that quotas of each overlay are not
violated;
•migrate VMs, to handle errors and faults, to reduce the
communication latency or to balance the computational
load;
•setup, compile and deliver to a STO-VM the general
security policy it has to enforce.
This overlay is also concerned with data management issues
because it has to map all the overlays so that each com-
munity has a fast access to its data. Alternative mapping
strategies may evenly distribute APP-VMs on the available
nodes, or map COM-VMs onto the physical nodes directly
connected to those that run the APP-VMs of the correspond-
ing overlay. The management overlay can also manage the
association among users and privileges in a centralized way
and implement a proper authentication protocol, so that users
can log on APP-VMs with the same combination of user-
name and password anywhere in the infrastructure. In this
way, the set of users that share an infrastructure is globally
known so that Vinci can uniquely identify users through their
user-name or their associated user identiﬁer, which is paired
with the security level of the user, i.e. with its privileges.
Whenever a user has been authenticated and has chosen
the community she wants to join, the INF-VM starts up
an APP-VM, which runs only those applications that satisfy
the community policy and with the proper global level to
run the user application. Then, the INF-VM contacts the
proper CC-VMs to insert this APP-VM into an overlay and
to add communication rules to handle the corresponding
information ﬂows. Finally, the INF-VM may update the
security policies of COM-VMs and FS-VMs. Currently,
INF-VMs are assigned to Xen Domain 0 VMs, i.e. they
are privileged VMs that can access the control interface to
manage a physical node and the VMs that the node runs.
IV. PER FO RM AN CE RE SU LTS
This section shows a preliminary performance evaluation
of the current Vinci prototype. The tests were performed
on several machines equipped with Intel Core 2 Duo E6550
2.33GHz CPUs. A ﬁrst experiment evaluated in an integrated
way the performance of ﬁle sharing through FS-VMs and
CC-VMs. An APP-VM on a node ran the IOzone [17] NFS
test while a FS-VM, on a distinct physical node, stored the
requested ﬁles. Requests were transmitted along a commu-
nication channel implemented by two Firewall VMs and the
physical nodes were connected through a 100MB Ethernet.
The overhead due to the enforcement of the security policies,
in the average case, is lower than 9%. Instead, the tests on
the enforcement of security policies by a COM-VM resulted
in a reduction of the ﬁnal throughput lower than 5%. The
same tests executed on an APP-VM connected to a remote
FS-VM through two VPN VMs resulted in an overhead that,
in the worst case, is lower than 13%.
We have also evaluated the overhead arising when an A-
VM attests and monitors the software of another VM. While
the attestation overhead is almost negligible, the monitoring
overhead depends upon the complexity of the checks that
are applied in a range that goes from 5% to 50%.
V. RE LATE D WOR KS
Several applications of cloud computing are discussed
in [18]. An extended review of risk assessment and man-
agement strategies for a cloud architecture is discussed
in [19]. [20] includes a detailed list of open issues. A
comprehensive discussion of the relations among virtual-
ization, security and multi-core architecture is presented
in [21]. Terra [22] is a VM-based architecture for trusted
computing to share commodity hardware among applications
with distinct security requirements. The software stack in
each VM can be tailored to meet the security requirements
of its applications. PlanetLab [23] is a global overlay net-
work that runs concurrently multiple services in slices, i.e.
networks of VMs that include some amount of processing,
memory, storage and network resources. PlanetLab exploits
the concept of an open grid of machines where resources
can be dynamically allocated and discovered. Poly2[24]
is a framework to segregate applications and networks and
to minimize OSes. The architecture maps network services
onto different systems and it isolates speciﬁc classes of net-
work trafﬁc. To this purpose, administrative and application-
speciﬁc trafﬁc are mapped onto distinct networks. Moreover,
minimized OSes only provide the services required by a
speciﬁc network application. Shamon [25] is an approach to
securing distributed computation based on a shared reference
monitor that enforces MAC policies across a distributed
set of machines. Shamon enables the reference monitors
on these machines to achieve a set of monitor guarantees.
SnowFlock [26] is a Xen-based implementation of the VM
fork abstraction that enables cloud users and programmers to
instantiate several VMs in different hosts in sub-second time,
with little run-time overhead. VM-FIT [27] is an architecture
that applies virtualization to build fault and intrusion tolerant
network-based services. The VM-FIT infrastructure inter-
cepts the client/service interaction at the hypervisor level,
below the guest OS that hosts a service, and distributes
requests to a replica group. [28] proposes a mechanism to
provide secure and efﬁcient access to large-scale outsourced
data, by encrypting every data block with a different key
to achieve ﬂexible cryptography-based access control. It
exploits key derivation methods so that the owner has
to maintain only a few secrets. Moreover, over-encryption
and/or lazy revocation prevent revoked users from getting
access to updated data blocks. [29] adopts virtualization to
monitor and protect the systems running in a cloud from
a centralized security VM. The proposed solution does not
assume any a-priori semantic knowledge of the guest OS or
any trust assumptions into the state of the VM. [30] proposes
a cryptographic network ﬁle system based on a MAC tree
construction that uses a universal-hash based stateful MAC
that results in standard model security proof and in better
performance than a Merkle hash tree. The implementation
is based on coreFS, a user-level network ﬁle system. [31]
proposes an image management system that controls access
to cloud’s image repository, tracks the provenance of images,
and provides users and administrators with efﬁcient image
ﬁlters and scanners that detect and repair security violations.
VI. CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WO RK S
In this paper, we have discussed the introduction of highly
parallel overlays of VMs to simplify the management of a
cloud infrastructure and increase the overall robustness. To
this end, Vinci introduces overlays that interconnect special-
ized VMs and where a VM may (i) support some applica-
tions (ii) protect either a shared ﬁle system or the other VMs.
Each overlay supports a distinct cloud community, i.e. a set
of users with similar security and reliability requirements.
A further overlay manages the cloud infrastructure and its
VMs can allocate and migrate the various VMs to achieve
the required levels of reliability and security.
An area of future research is focused on protection against
physical attacks so that the cloud provider and the cloud
administrators do not have to be trusted. We are also in-
vestigating how redundancy can be transparently introduced
into an overlay to mask faults of hardware components
without migrating a VM. Another critical issue is the adop-
tion of multi-core architecture with virtualization support to
minimize the overhead introduced by virtualization and the
one due to the large number of VMs. Lastly, the complete
integration of Vinci with the trusted computing framework
supports the deﬁnition of a root-of-trust for the assurance
checks of the A-VMs.
